final_rationale: The BFS accepts a cell using a faulty local condition based on static passable-neighbor counts rather than the current forced expansion condition. That rejects corridor cells that should become '+', leaving most of the row unfilled.

The first witness actual path is also a valid minimum jump sequence, so the exact mismatch does not demonstrate an output-format bug. The best-supported concern is the custom 0-1 BFS and segment-graph reconstruction design, but this item remains ambiguous.
The tree-game code uses the Euler-tour index returned by qry as if it were a node id in vis/fa and subtree updates. That index mix-up corrupts the selected path and produces an impossible score.

The centroid structure compares answer pairs lexicographically as (enjoyment,toll), so equal enjoyment prefers a larger toll. The failing query should keep the starting city with toll 0 but returns toll 1.

The first witness paths appear to be valid and make all used edge parities even, so the exact mismatch does not show a concrete output-format failure. The closest supported concern is the custom tree-path parity construction, but the evidence is ambiguous.

The submitted combinatorial DP fills a table with a recurrence that does not match the simultaneous-damage no-winner process. The state table is present, but the transition formula produces the wrong count.
The feasibility logic mishandles the n=1, k=0 boundary and accepts a graph whose diameter 0 is not strictly less than -1. This is a special input-boundary case in the condition set.

The first public witness prints a valid independent set, so the exact mismatch is non-unique-output ambiguity. The code nevertheless relies on incomplete randomized sampling after failing to exhaustively search both possible party types.
The comparison normalization subtracts digit-length differences and then compares only a shared prefix. It ignores remaining significant digits, declaring 1201 and 1200 equal.

The internal sort simulation moves the selected element to the end of the segment, equivalent to a left shift by 1, but it prints offset i-id. The emitted operations do not reproduce the sorted array.

The first witness actual path is also a valid minimum jump sequence, so the exact mismatch does not demonstrate an output-format bug. The best-supported concern is the custom 0-1 BFS and segment-graph reconstruction design, but this item remains ambiguous.

The code reconstructs the first addend interval from a zero-based stored end position without converting correctly to one-based bounds. It outputs 1 6 instead of the valid 2 7, an off-by-one substring boundary error.
The feasibility condition rejects valid extrema counts unless a+b is almost n-1. Thus valid sample cases such as n=4, a=1, b=1 are sent to the -1 branch.

The rectangle test reduces the condition to one stick equaling the sum of the other two. It misses the mathematical case where two sticks are already equal and the remaining even stick can be split into two equal sides.
After sorting candidates by occurrence count in increasing order, the query returns sorted_occurrences[size-k] instead of the k-th element from the front. That reversed index selects the wrong value.
The DP state counts increasing health totals and does not represent arena survival states or the no-winner condition. As a result it returns 0 for a case where every assignment is no-winner.
The program minimizes and reports the number of changed dishes rather than the number of distinct resulting dishes. It therefore misses the stated global variety objective.

The program reads only two of the four side point lists, so later side data is parsed as later test cases. Independently, it computes adjacent gaps instead of side span times the opposite rectangle dimension.
When both AB and BA occur, the code always edits a BA occurrence instead of first checking whether the counts are already equal. It changes an already valid sample string.
The solution uses floating products and ratio counts rather than exact number-theoretic divisibility information. It loses the prime-factor structure needed to count simple segments.
The feasibility condition for the peak/valley permutation is over-strict and rejects valid sample cases. The local boolean test sends feasible inputs to the -1 branch.
The DP state counts bounded integer compositions by total health, not outcomes of the simultaneous-damage arena process. It cannot represent the no-winner condition and returns 0 for the sample.
The program prints divisors of n regardless of the string contents or which multiples already contain the target character. It misunderstands how the operation preserves positions divisible by x.
The implementation abandons the general k>1 cyclic segment counting algorithm and returns without computing an answer. The empty output is a consequence of the unfinished algorithmic branch.
The stick-splitting derivation only checks whether the broken stick can be split into the other two lengths. It misses the equal-unbroken-sticks case such as 5 5 4.
The hard tree-coloring solver stops after building relevant nodes and returns before performing the DP/counting step. It never implements the required general algorithm.
The tree-game DP tracks an invented activated-node quantity rather than the minimax score components needed for Red and Blue play. That state overestimates the achievable score on the path sample.
The ticket-query solver is a placeholder that prints a[l] for each query after abandoning the interval DP. It ignores later cheaper tickets and validity windows.
The explicit k == n branch returns n-1, but this boundary still needs the initial doubling spread; for n=6,k=6 the correct answer is 3. The failure is incorrect handling of that boundary case.
The combinatorial derivation for cyclic difficulty plans is abandoned and the fallback prints 0. The code never implements a valid counting formula for the threshold-adjacency cycle.
The type-2 permutation query handler is left unfinished inside the query loop. It consumes the queries but never computes the required permutation iterates.
The stick-splitting test omits the case where two unbroken sticks are equal and the remaining stick can be split into two equal parts. It rejects 5 5 4 even though a rectangle is possible.

The first witness paths appear valid and make all used edge parities even, so the exact mismatch does not prove a concrete failure. The closest supported concern is the simplified tree-path construction, but the evidence is ambiguous.
The deque inversion minimization is abandoned and the program prints 0 for every test case. It misses nonzero inversion minima rather than applying a flawed local choice.
The code accepts the n=1, m=0, k=0 boundary even though the graph diameter 0 is not strictly less than -1. The single-node case is handled too broadly for small k.
The arena counting derivation is unfinished and the program falls back to 0 for all n>2. It never implements the combinatorial formula needed to count no-winner health assignments.
